revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Shinto_Shrine#Shinmei-zukuri]]
#REDIRECT [[Shinto_shrine#Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]]

[[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{nihongo|''Shinmei-zukuri''|神明造}} is an ancient ''[[honden]]'' style typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]], the holiest of Shinto shrines.<ref name="ko">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> It is most common in Mie prefecture.<ref name="jashi">JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009</ref>
Like the ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]'' styles, its birth predates the arrival of [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism]]. Characterized by an extreme simplicity, its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture. Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and decorative logs called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'' protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
[[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise shrine's ''honden'']]Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, they are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]].<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>
==Notes== {{Reflist}} {{Japan-art-stub}} {{arch-style-stub}}
[[ja:神明造]] <nowiki>[[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]</nowiki>

Characterized by an extreme simplicity, its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture. Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and decorative logs called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'' protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
[[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise shrine's ''honden'']]Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. [[ja:神明造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Shinmei-zukuri'''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese architectural style typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[Shinto shrines]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in Mie prefecture.<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikine sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day..<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure= This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity, its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and decorative logs called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'' protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
[[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise shrine's ''honden'']]Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]]<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>, which gives the name to the style.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=144377281X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0804838380 }}
[[ja:神明造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Shinmei-zukuri'''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese architectural style typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[Shinto shrines]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in Mie prefecture.<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikine sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity, its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and decorative logs called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'' protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
[[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise shrine's ''honden'']]Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]]<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>, which gives the name to the style.

In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|left|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity, its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and decorative logs called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'' protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>

The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]]<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>, which gives the name to the style.

[[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Shinmei-zukuri'''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese architectural style typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[Shinto shrines]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in Mie prefecture.<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|left|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity, its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and decorative logs called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' and ''[[katsuogi]]'' protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]]<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>, which gives the name to the style.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=144377281X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0804838380 }}
[[ja:神明造]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Japanese architecture]]

[[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Shinmei-zukuri'''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese architectural style typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[Shinto shrines]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in Mie prefecture.<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'' and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi Taisha]]<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/>
==Structure== [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|left|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in planed, unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''hirairi'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]]<ref name="yahoo">[http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref>, which gives the name to the style.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&dq=shinto+architecture&lr=&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=144377281X }}, *{{cite book |last= Young |first= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=http://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&dq=shinto+architecture&as_brr=3&source=gbs_navlinks_s |accessdate=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |origyear=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=0804838380 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q930545]] Wikidata name: Shinmei-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese architectural style}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''''Shinmei-zukuri'''''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'', and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]],<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively, and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]
==Structure== This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in plane-unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one of the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.
==See also== *[[:en:Austronesian architecture|Austronesian architecture]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q930545]] Wikidata name: Shinmei-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese architectural style}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''''Shinmei-zukuri'''''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'', and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]],<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively, and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]
==Structure== This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in plane-unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one of the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.
==See also== *[[:en:Austronesian architecture|Austronesian architecture]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Shinmeizukuri]] [[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]] [[ja:神明造]] [[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]] [[zh:神明造]] {{wikidata link|Q930545}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]
{{wikidata link|Q930545}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q930545]] Wikidata name: Shinmei-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese architectural style}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''''Shinmei-zukuri'''''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'', and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]],<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively, and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]
==Structure== This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in plane-unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one of the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.
==See also== *[[:en:Austronesian architecture|Austronesian architecture]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]

Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q930545]] Wikidata name: Shinmei-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese architectural style}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''''Shinmei-zukuri'''''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'', and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]],<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively, and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]
==Structure== This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in plane-unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one of the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.
==See also== *[[:en:Austronesian architecture|Austronesian architecture]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q930545]] Wikidata name: Shinmei-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese architectural style}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''''Shinmei-zukuri'''''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'', and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]],<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively, and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]
==Structure== This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in plane-unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one of the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.

[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Shinmeizukuri]] [[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]] [[ja:神明造]] [[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]] [[zh:神明造]]
{{wikidata link|Q930545}} [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Shinmei-zukuri]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q930545]] Wikidata name: Shinmei-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese architectural style}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''''Shinmei-zukuri'''''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'', and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]],<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively, and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]
==Structure== This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in plane-unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one of the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.
==See also== *[[:en:Austronesian architecture|Austronesian architecture]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q930545]] Wikidata name: Shinmei-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese architectural style}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''''Shinmei-zukuri'''''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese [[:en:Shinto architecture|architectural style]] typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrines]] were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were [[:en:Thatching|thatched]] with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'', and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]],<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively, and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]
==Structure== This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in plane-unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one of the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''[[:en:Hirairi|Hirairi]]'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''[[:en:Chigi (architecture)|chigi]]'' (vertical) and ''[[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]]'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.
==See also== *[[:en:Austronesian architecture|Austronesian architecture]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]

Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q930545]] Wikidata name: Shinmei-zukuri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese architectural style}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''''Shinmei-zukuri'''''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style}} typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}.<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}.<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines}} were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|WD=Q874531|lt=thatched}} with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'', and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]],<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively, and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]
==Structure== This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in plane-unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one of the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi}}'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.
==See also== *[[:en:Austronesian architecture|Austronesian architecture]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}

In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]

The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Shinmei-zukuri]][[es:Shinmeizukuri]][[fr:Shinmei-zukuri]][[ja:神明造]][[ru:Симмэй-дзукури]][[zh:神明造]]
{{Short description|Japanese architectural style}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:IseShrineBuilding.jpg|thumb|275 px|A building at Ise Shrine]] {{Nihongo|'''''Shinmei-zukuri'''''|神明造}} is an ancient Japanese {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=architectural style|1=Shinto architecture}} typical of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]'s ''[[honden]]'', the holiest of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}.<ref name="ko">Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> It is most common in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}.<ref name="jashi">JAANUS</ref>
==History== Ancient {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} were constructed according to the style of dwellings ([[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]])<ref name="YY">Young & Young (2007:50)</ref><ref name="ki33">Kishida (2008:33)</ref> or storehouses ([[Ise Grand Shrine]]).<ref name="YY"/><ref name="fc">Fletcher and Cruickshank (1996:724)</ref> The buildings had [[:en:Gabled roof|Gabled roof]]s, raised floors, plank walls, and were {{ill|Thatching|en|Thatching|WD=Q874531|lt=thatched|1=Thatching}} with reed or covered with [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|hinoki cypress]] bark.<ref name="fc"/> Such early shrines did not include a space for worship.<ref name="YY"/> Three important forms of ancient shrine architectural styles exist: ''[[taisha-zukuri]]'', ''shinmei-zukuri'', and ''[[sumiyoshi-zukuri]]''<ref name="ki34">Kishida (2008:34)</ref> They are exemplified by [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]], [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]] and [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]],<ref name="ki35">Kishida (2008:35)</ref> respectively, and date to before 552.<ref name="ki126">Kishida (2008:126)</ref> According to the tradition of {{nihongo|''Shikinen sengū-sai|式年遷宮祭}}'', the buildings or shrines were faithfully rebuilt at regular intervals adhering to the original design. In this manner, ancient styles have been replicated through the centuries to the present day.<ref name="ki33"/> [[File:Ise Shrine Meizukuri.jpg|thumb|Ise Shrine's ''honden'']]
==Structure== This style is characterized by an extreme simplicity. Its basic features can be seen in Japanese architecture from the [[Kofun period]] (250–538 C.E.) onwards and it is considered the pinnacle of Japanese traditional architecture.<ref name="ko"/> Built in plane-unfinished wood, the ''honden'' is either 3x2 ''ken'' or 1x1''ken'' in size, has a raised floor, a gabled roof<ref>A gable is the generally triangular portion of a wall enclosed between the edges of a sloping roof.</ref> with an entry on one of the non-gabled sides ({{nihongo|''{{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|WD=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi|1=Hirairi}}'' or ''hirairi-zukuri''|平入・平入造}}), no upward curve at the eaves, and purely decorative logs called ''{{ill|Chigi (architecture)|en|Chigi (architecture)|WD=Q262282|lt=chigi|1=Chigi (architecture)}}'' (vertical) and ''{{ill|Katsuogi|en|Katsuogi|WD=Q13479730|lt=Katsuogi|1=Katsuogi}}'' (horizontal) protruding from the roof's ridge.<ref name="jashi"/>
Because shrines used to be rebuilt every 20 years, as Ise Shrine still is, shrines of this type are mostly of recent construction. The oldest extant example is [[Nishina Shinmei Shrine]],<ref name="yahoo">[https://archive.today/20121209043808/http://100.yahoo.co.jp/detail/%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E5%BB%BA%E7%AF%89/ Jinja Kenchiku], [[Shogakukan]] Nihon Daihyakka Zensho, accessed on November 29, 2009</ref> which gives the name to the style.
==See also== *[[:en:Austronesian architecture|Austronesian architecture]]
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
==References== *JAANUS, [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/s/shinmeizukuri.htm Shinmei-zukuri] accessed on December 1, 2009 *[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=248 History and Typology of Shrine Architecture], Encyclopedia of Shinto accessed on November 29, 2009 *{{cite book |last= Kishida |first= Hideto |title= Japanese Architecture |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gcJKAIm6HAEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2008 |publisher=READ BOOKS |isbn=978-1-4437-7281-5 }}, *{{cite book |last1= Young |first1= David |last2=Young |first2=Michiko |title= The art of Japanese architecture |series=Architecture and Interior Design |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Ag5WpEKifGEC&q=shinto+architecture |access-date=2009-11-11 |year=2007 |orig-year=2004 |edition=illustrated, revised |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3838-2 }}
